responses.strategic_rationale: The project must balance rapid participant enrollment, high‑quality data capture, and sustainable operations within a limited budget. Core tensions arise between expanding recruitment reach and maintaining rigorous pre‑screening, between staffing flexibility and consistent expertise, and between automation speed and annotation accuracy. By targeting levers that directly influence recruitment channels, shift design, annotation workflow, algorithm validation, compensation, and safety protocols, the plan addresses these conflicts while preserving the residential, naturalistic environment essential for capturing rare parasomnia events.

levers.name: Recruitment Channel Diversification
levers.consequences: Expanding recruitment beyond hospital and neurologist referrals will increase participant diversity and enrollment speed, but may introduce variability in pre‑screening quality and increase administrative burden.
options: Partner with local sleep clinics across Germany to funnel referrals into the Bonn unit.
Launch a targeted online campaign using patient forums and social media to identify self‑referred candidates.
Offer a mobile screening van that visits community centers to conduct preliminary interviews and EEG headband tests.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: Balancing depth of clinical screening with breadth of recruitment. Weakness: Options overlook the need for consistent pre‑admission eligibility verification.

levers.name: Staffing Shift Rotation Optimization
levers.consequences: Implementing a rotating shift schedule for technicians will reduce fatigue and improve event response times, but may increase overtime costs and complicate training consistency.
options: Adopt a 12‑hour on/12‑hour off rotation with staggered start times to cover all suites.
Introduce a cross‑training program where technicians alternate between night and day duties to maintain skill diversity.
Deploy a part‑time night supervisor who oversees multiple suites simultaneously to streamline oversight.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: Ensuring continuous coverage while managing staff well‑being. Weakness: Options fail to address potential skill dilution from frequent role changes.

levers.name: Annotation Workflow Automation
levers.consequences: Automating initial event flagging will accelerate annotation throughput and reduce human error, yet may miss subtle events and increase false positives requiring manual review.
options: Integrate a rule‑based threshold detector that flags rapid arousal patterns for technician review.
Use a lightweight machine learning model trained on pilot data to pre‑sort events before human scoring.
Implement a semi‑automatic annotation interface that highlights probable events but requires technician confirmation.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: Speeding annotation versus maintaining accuracy. Weakness: Options do not consider the impact on inter‑rater reliability metrics.

levers.name: Event‑Triage Algorithm Validation
levers.consequences: Establishing a rigorous validation protocol will enhance confidence in the triage tool, but may delay deployment and increase resource demands for dual scoring.
options: Conduct a blinded comparison study where the algorithm’s output is scored by two independent raters before acceptance.
Run a live pilot where the algorithm flags events in real time and technicians confirm or reject them, recording outcomes for later analysis.
Create a synthetic dataset of simulated parasomnia events to benchmark algorithm performance before clinical use.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: Validating algorithm performance versus accelerating research timelines. Weakness: Options overlook the need for continuous post‑deployment monitoring.

levers.name: Participant Compensation Structure
levers.consequences: Adjusting compensation levels can improve recruitment rates and participant satisfaction, but may strain the budget and raise ethical concerns about coercion.
options: Offer a flat nightly stipend plus a bonus for completing the full stay duration.
Provide a tiered payment system where participants receive incremental increases for each additional week they stay.
Include non‑monetary incentives such as free sleep hygiene workshops and personalized sleep reports.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: Incentivizing participation while preserving ethical standards. Weakness: Options ignore potential disparities in perceived value across demographic groups.

levers.name: Facility Safety and Privacy Protocols
levers.consequences: Strengthening safety and privacy measures will protect participants and staff, but may increase renovation costs and reduce the domestic feel of the suites.
options: Install biometric access controls for each suite to limit entry to authorized personnel only.
Deploy a real‑time monitoring system that alerts staff to any breach of safety thresholds (e.g., window opening, fire alarms).
Use privacy curtains and sound masking in each suite to minimize video capture while preserving naturalistic behavior.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: Maintaining a naturalistic environment versus ensuring participant safety and data privacy. Weakness: Options fail to address the trade‑off between safety features and participant comfort.

responses.summary: Add 'Offer a mobile screening van that visits community centers to conduct preliminary interviews and EEG headband tests' to Recruitment Channel Diversification.

responses.strategic_rationale: The project hinges on capturing rare parasomnia events in a residential setting, demanding rigorous data integrity, participant engagement, and ethical oversight while scaling infrastructure and sharing findings responsibly. These levers target the core tensions between operational efficiency, data quality, participant retention, regulatory compliance, and scientific visibility, ensuring that each decision supports the 36‑month success criteria without compromising safety or research integrity.

levers.name: Longitudinal Data Integrity Assurance
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever guarantees that nightly recordings remain complete and uncorrupted, reducing the need for re‑capture and preserving statistical power. However, it increases nightly monitoring overhead and requires additional staff training on verification protocols.
options: Deploy a nightly automated checksum verification system that flags corrupted files for immediate re‑capture.
Introduce a manual audit trail where technicians log any sensor anomalies during each shift.
Establish a redundancy protocol that mirrors data to a secondary NAS in real time to guard against hardware failure.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: Data completeness versus operational burden. Weakness: Options overlook the impact of network latency on real‑time mirroring.

levers.name: Participant Retention Incentive Design
levers.consequences: Adjusting incentives can extend participant stays, yielding richer longitudinal data, but may also influence self‑reporting and introduce bias. The trade‑off is between longer data collection windows and the risk of altered participant behavior.
options: Offer tiered meal vouchers that increase with each consecutive night stayed.
Provide a post‑study wellness package that includes a sleep hygiene kit and a follow‑up consultation.
Implement a progress badge system that rewards participants for completing nightly questionnaires.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: Retention incentives versus participant authenticity. Weakness: Options ignore potential bias introduced by reward‑driven reporting.

levers.name: Ethics Compliance Scalability
levers.consequences: Scaling ethics oversight ensures regulatory adherence and participant trust, but may slow recruitment and increase administrative load. The downstream effect is a more robust compliance framework at the cost of slower study initiation.
options: Create a dedicated ethics liaison role that coordinates approvals across all study sites.
Develop a modular consent template that can be adapted quickly for new participant sub‑groups.
Automate ethics audit logs using a secure spreadsheet that tracks all protocol deviations.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: Rapid scaling versus thorough ethics review. Weakness: Options fail to address the need for real‑time monitoring of adverse events.

levers.name: Infrastructure Scalability Planning
levers.consequences: Planning for future suite expansion reduces downtime during growth, but requires upfront capital and careful coordination with building codes. The trade‑off is smoother transition to 12 suites versus higher initial expenditure.
options: Lease modular, pre‑fitted bedroom units that can be installed within a month.
Retrofit the existing building with expandable electrical and network conduits for future suites.
Partner with a local construction firm to design a phased expansion that aligns with funding milestones.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: Immediate capacity versus long‑term scalability. Weakness: Options overlook the impact of building code compliance on expansion speed.

levers.name: Data Governance and Sharing Strategy
levers.consequences: Defining governance clarifies data ownership and sharing, enhancing trust but potentially limiting external collaboration. The downstream implication is clearer rights but a higher administrative burden for each data request.
options: Adopt a tiered data access model that grants researchers varying levels of detail based on contribution.
Implement a data use agreement template that requires anonymization and a signed statement of intent.
Create a data stewardship committee that reviews all external requests before approval.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: Data openness versus privacy protection. Weakness: Options do not consider the administrative burden of ongoing data requests.

levers.name: Research Output Dissemination Plan
levers.consequences: A structured dissemination plan increases visibility and citation potential, yet may divert resources from core data collection. The trade‑off is higher publication rate versus potential dilution of research focus.
options: Assign a dedicated science communication officer to coordinate conference presentations and manuscript submissions.
Schedule quarterly internal review meetings to align research milestones with publication timelines.
Leverage open‑access preprint servers to share preliminary findings with the community.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: Rapid dissemination versus research depth. Weakness: Options ignore the need for peer‑review quality control.

responses.summary: Add 'implement a real‑time data checksum verification system' to Longitudinal Data Integrity Assurance.

responses.strategic_rationale: The project hinges on balancing naturalistic data capture with stringent safety, efficient annotation, transparent algorithmic decision‑making, participant autonomy, real‑time safety monitoring, and robust control data integration. These levers directly address the core tensions between ecological validity and operational risk, human resource constraints and data quality, algorithmic opacity and regulatory compliance, participant trust and administrative burden, alert fatigue and safety assurance, and baseline comparability versus resource allocation. By targeting these dimensions, the plan can navigate the trade‑offs among speed, cost, scope, and quality that define the feasibility of a residential parasomnia research unit.

levers.name: Participant Sleep Environment Authenticity
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will adjust the balance between a domestic feel and clinical safety features. Enhancing authenticity may reduce participant stress and improve naturalistic event capture, but could increase risk of accidental injury or data loss if safety measures are relaxed. The design choice will influence both participant compliance and the integrity of physiological recordings.
options: Increase acoustic insulation and use soft furnishings to mimic a home setting while maintaining padded corridors.
Implement a hybrid design with clinical safety features hidden behind decorative panels to preserve safety without overt clinical cues.
Deploy a modular room concept that can be reconfigured between a clinical and a home‑like layout depending on study phase.
levers.review_lever: Balances naturalistic sleep environment against safety protocols. Weakness: The options overlook the impact of environmental noise on event detection accuracy.

levers.name: Data Annotation Workforce Scalability
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will influence how annotation workload is distributed among technicians and raters. Expanding the workforce can reduce per‑night annotation time and improve inter‑rater reliability, but increases personnel costs and may dilute expertise. The chosen strategy will determine the speed and consistency of event adjudication.
options: Recruit part‑time graduate students to perform preliminary annotation under supervision to free senior technicians for complex cases.
Introduce a tiered annotation workflow where automated pre‑labeling guides technicians, reducing manual effort.
Implement a rotating shift system that allows technicians to work in pairs, ensuring continuous coverage and peer review.
levers.review_lever: Controls annotation throughput versus staff expertise. Weakness: The options do not address potential variability introduced by non‑expert annotators.

levers.name: Event Detection Algorithm Transparency
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will affect how algorithmic decisions are communicated to reviewers. Greater transparency can improve trust and facilitate debugging, but may expose sensitive intermediate data and increase processing time. The level of transparency will shape the collaboration between computational and clinical teams.
options: Provide a visual heatmap of sensor contributions for each flagged event to aid human reviewers.
Publish a simplified decision tree that summarizes the algorithm’s key thresholds and logic.
Offer a live dashboard that shows real‑time confidence scores and allows reviewers to adjust thresholds interactively.
levers.review_lever: Balances algorithmic opacity against reviewer confidence. Weakness: The options ignore the need for regulatory compliance regarding data handling.

levers.name: Dynamic Consent Management
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will change how participants can modify their data sharing preferences over time. Allowing dynamic consent can increase participant trust and data utility, but may complicate data governance and require additional administrative overhead. The approach will determine the flexibility and integrity of the data ecosystem.
options: Implement a web portal where participants can update consent preferences for each data type at any time.
Use a tiered consent model that pre‑sets common preferences but allows overrides during follow‑up visits.
Embed consent prompts within the nightly check‑in process to capture real‑time preferences.
levers.review_lever: Controls participant autonomy versus administrative complexity. Weakness: The options fail to consider the impact on long‑term data linkage consistency.

levers.name: Real‑Time Safety Alert System
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will introduce automated monitoring of safety indicators during participant stays. Real‑time alerts can reduce injury risk and improve staff responsiveness, but may increase false alarms and staff workload. The system design will influence the safety culture and operational efficiency of the unit.
options: Deploy motion sensors that trigger alerts when a participant exits a designated safe zone during the night.
Use physiological thresholds (e.g., heart rate spikes) to flag potential distress events for immediate review.
Combine environmental sensors with a rule‑based system that escalates alerts only when multiple criteria are met simultaneously.
levers.review_lever: Balances safety vigilance against alert fatigue. Weakness: The options do not address the calibration of alert thresholds to individual participant baselines.

levers.name: Matched Control Data Integration
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will influence how control data is incorporated into analyses. Integrating control data can improve baseline modeling and algorithm benchmarking, but may require additional resources for matching and storage. The integration strategy will affect the validity of comparative findings.
options: Align control participants’ stay durations with those of cases to ensure comparable data volumes.
Use propensity score matching to pair controls with cases based on demographic and sleep metrics.
Store control data in a separate, secure repository to maintain clear separation from case data.
levers.review_lever: Controls data comparability versus resource allocation. Weakness: The options overlook the potential bias introduced by differential data quality between groups.

responses.summary: Add 'deploy motion sensors that trigger alerts when a participant exits a designated safe zone during the night' to Real‑Time Safety Alert System.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever` (one field, two sentences):
     First sentence names the core tension. Second sentence identifies a weakness.
     Example: "Controls centralization vs. local autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to account for transition costs."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.
   - For `summary`:
     One sentence prescribing a concrete addition to a specific lever.
     Example: "Add 'partner with a regional distributor for last-mile logistics' to Supply Chain Strategy."

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach — not a vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
